quiz_questions.question_text: What is accuracy in measurement?

options.text: How close a measured value is to the true (accepted) value
The type of instrument used for measurement
How close repeated measurements are to each other
The number of decimal places in a measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: Accuracy describes how close a measurement is to the actual or accepted value. A measurement can be precise (consistent) but inaccurate (far from the true value).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is precision in measurement?

options.text: How close repeated measurements are to each other
How close a measurement is to the true value
The speed at which measurements are taken
The total number of measurements taken

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision refers to the reproducibility of measurements. Highly precise measurements cluster closely together, even if they are all far from the true value.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many significant figures are in the number 0.00450?
quiz_questions.explanation: Leading zeros are not significant. The significant digits are 4, 5, and the trailing 0 (which is significant because it comes after the decimal point and after a nonzero digit). So 0.00450 has 3 significant figures.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many significant figures are in the number 1200?

options.text: 2 (unless a decimal point is shown)

quiz_questions.explanation: Trailing zeros in a whole number without a decimal point are ambiguous but typically considered not significant. So 1200 has 2 significant figures (1 and 2). Writing 1200. or 1.200 × 10³ clarifies that all four digits are significant.

quiz_questions.question_text: When multiplying or dividing measurements, how do you determine the number of significant figures in the result?

options.text: The result should have the sum of all significant figures
Always use the most significant figures available
Significant figures don't matter in multiplication
The result should have the same number of significant figures as the measurement with the fewest significant figures

quiz_questions.explanation: In multiplication and division, the result is limited by the measurement with the fewest significant figures. For example, 4.56 × 1.4 = 6.4 (not 6.384) because 1.4 has only 2 significant figures.

quiz_questions.question_text: When adding or subtracting measurements, how do you determine the significant figures in the result?

options.text: Round to one decimal place in all cases
Always keep all decimal places
The result should be rounded to the same number of decimal places as the measurement with the fewest decimal places
Use the same rule as multiplication — fewest significant figures

quiz_questions.explanation: In addition and subtraction, the result is rounded to the fewest decimal places of any measurement. For example, 12.11 + 18.0 = 30.1 (not 30.11) because 18.0 has only one decimal place.

quiz_questions.question_text: A measurement is both accurate and precise. What does this mean?

options.text: The measurement was taken only once
The measurements are consistently close to the true value and close to each other
The measurements use many significant figures
The instrument was very expensive

quiz_questions.explanation: When measurements are both accurate and precise, they cluster tightly around the true value. This is the ideal outcome of any measurement process.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many significant figures are in the number 50.030?
quiz_questions.explanation: All digits are significant: 5, 0, 0, 3, and the trailing 0. Zeros between nonzero digits are always significant, and the trailing zero after the decimal point is significant.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the purpose of significant figures in science?

options.text: To reduce the number of calculations needed
To communicate the precision of a measurement
To make numbers look cleaner in calculations
To make all measurements the same length

quiz_questions.explanation: Significant figures indicate how precisely a measurement was made. They communicate meaningful digits so that calculated results don't imply more precision than the original measurements warrant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Are leading zeros significant?

options.text: Only in scientific notation
Yes — all zeros are significant
Only if they come after a decimal point
No — leading zeros only serve as placeholders and are never significant

quiz_questions.explanation: Leading zeros (e.g., 0.0045) merely indicate the position of the decimal point. They are not significant. In 0.0045, only 4 and 5 are significant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes accuracy?

options.text: The range of values obtained from repeated measurements
The ability of an instrument to give consistent readings
The total number of decimal places in a measurement
The degree to which a measured value agrees with the true or accepted value of the quantity being measured

quiz_questions.explanation: Accuracy is about correctness — how close you are to the target. A single measurement can be accurate even if repeated measurements vary, and vice versa.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes precision?

options.text: How close a measurement is to the true value
The smallest increment on a measuring instrument
The degree of agreement among repeated measurements of the same quantity under the same conditions
The number of significant figures in a measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision reflects reproducibility. If you measure the same thing five times and get 5.01, 5.02, 5.01, 5.02, 5.01, the precision is high even if the true value is 6.00 (low accuracy).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a significant figure?

options.text: Any digit in a number
Only the first nonzero digit
A digit in a measurement that is known with certainty plus one estimated digit, indicating the precision of the measurement
The number of decimal places after the decimal point

quiz_questions.explanation: Significant figures include all reliably known digits plus the first uncertain (estimated) digit. They communicate the level of confidence in a measurement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes systematic error?

options.text: An error caused by rounding numbers
An error that varies randomly from measurement to measurement
An error that only occurs in digital instruments
A consistent, repeatable error that shifts all measurements in the same direction by the same amount, affecting accuracy

quiz_questions.explanation: Systematic errors produce consistent bias (all readings too high or too low). Causes include miscalibrated instruments, zero errors, and environmental factors. They affect accuracy but not precision.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes random error?

options.text: An error caused by using the wrong formula
An error that only occurs in analog instruments
An unpredictable variation in measurements caused by uncontrollable factors, affecting precision
An error that is consistently the same in every measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: Random errors cause measurements to scatter unpredictably around the true value. They might be caused by vibrations, temperature fluctuations, or human estimation. They affect precision but not accuracy systematically.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes percent error?

options.text: The number of significant figures expressed as a percentage
The precision of an instrument expressed as a percentage
The percentage of measurements that are wrong
The ratio of the absolute difference between a measured value and the accepted value to the accepted value, expressed as a percentage

quiz_questions.explanation: Percent error = |measured − accepted| / accepted × 100%. It quantifies how far a measurement is from the true value. A lower percent error indicates greater accuracy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes rounding?

options.text: Always removing the last digit of a number
Replacing all zeros with significant digits
Converting a number to scientific notation
The process of reducing the number of digits in a value while maintaining an approximation that is as close to the original as possible

quiz_questions.explanation: Rounding adjusts a number to a specified number of significant figures or decimal places. If the digit being removed is ≥ 5, round up; if < 5, round down.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes calibration?

options.text: Reading a measurement at eye level
Using the same instrument for every measurement
Taking the average of multiple measurements
The process of adjusting and verifying a measuring instrument against a known standard to ensure its readings are accurate

quiz_questions.explanation: Calibration eliminates systematic errors by comparing an instrument's readings to a known reference standard and adjusting accordingly. Regular calibration maintains measurement accuracy over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an exact number?

options.text: The average of many measurements
A number with more than 10 significant figures
A number with no uncertainty — it is known with complete certainty, such as counted quantities or defined conversions
A measurement taken with high-precision instruments

quiz_questions.explanation: Exact numbers have infinite significant figures. Examples: there are exactly 12 eggs in a dozen, and exactly 100 cm in 1 m. They do not limit significant figures in calculations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the resolution of an instrument?

options.text: The speed at which the instrument takes readings
The smallest change in a measured quantity that the instrument can detect
The largest measurement the instrument can make
The accuracy of the instrument

quiz_questions.explanation: Resolution (also called least count) is the smallest increment an instrument can display or detect. A ruler marked in millimeters has 1 mm resolution. Higher resolution allows more precise measurements.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the length of a desk five times: 1.52 m, 1.53 m, 1.52 m, 1.52 m, 1.53 m. The actual length is 1.50 m. How would you describe these measurements?

options.text: Neither accurate nor precise — the measurements are unreliable
Both accurate and precise — they are consistent and close to the true value
Accurate but not precise — they are close to the true value but vary widely
Precise but not accurate — the measurements are consistent with each other but systematically higher than the true value

quiz_questions.explanation: The measurements cluster tightly (1.52–1.53 m range = high precision) but are consistently above the true value of 1.50 m (low accuracy). This pattern indicates a systematic error, perhaps a bent ruler or incorrect technique.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemistry student measures the density of aluminum three times: 2.70, 2.68, and 2.72 g/cm³. The accepted value is 2.70 g/cm³. How should these measurements be described?

options.text: Accurate but not precise — the average is correct but the values are spread out
Neither accurate nor precise — more measurements are needed
Both accurate and precise — the average (2.70 g/cm³) matches the accepted value and the measurements are closely clustered
Precise but not accurate — the values are close together but far from the true value

quiz_questions.explanation: The measurements are closely grouped (precision) and their average equals the accepted value (accuracy). This represents the ideal outcomes of careful measurement technique and properly calibrated equipment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student calculates the area of a rectangular room by multiplying 4.5 m × 3.72 m and reports the area as 16.740 m². Their teacher marks this incorrect. Why?

options.text: The student should have added the measurements instead of multiplying
The area should be reported in square centimeters
The teacher made an error — 16.740 m² is correct
The answer should have only 2 significant figures (17 m²) because 4.5 has only 2 significant figures, and in multiplication the result is limited to the fewest sig figs

quiz_questions.explanation: In multiplication, the result must have the same number of significant figures as the least precise input. Here, 4.5 has 2 sig figs and 3.72 has 3 sig figs. The result should be rounded to 2 sig figs: 17 m².

quiz_questions.question_text: A digital thermometer consistently reads 2.0°C higher than the actual temperature. A student takes five readings: 22.0, 22.1, 21.9, 22.0, 22.0°C (actual temperature is 20.0°C). What type of error is present?

options.text: Random error — the readings vary slightly
No error — the thermometer is working correctly
Human error — the student is reading the thermometer wrong
Systematic error — the thermometer is miscalibrated, causing all readings to be shifted by the same amount from the true value

quiz_questions.explanation: The consistent +2.0°C offset is a systematic error caused by poor calibration. The slight variation (21.9–22.1) represents additional random error. Calibrating the thermometer against a standard would correct the systematic error.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student weighs an object: 5.00 g. She then measures its volume: 2.1 mL. She calculates the density as 5.00 / 2.1 = 2.380952... g/mL and reports 2.380952 g/mL. What is wrong with her reported density?

options.text: She should report all the decimal places from the calculator
The units are incorrect
The result should be rounded to 2 significant figures (2.4 g/mL) because 2.1 mL has only 2 significant figures
She divided when she should have multiplied

quiz_questions.explanation: In division, the result is limited to the fewest significant figures of any input. 5.00 g has 3 sig figs; 2.1 mL has 2 sig figs. The density should be reported as 2.4 g/mL — reporting extra digits falsely implies greater precision.

quiz_questions.question_text: An archer shoots five arrows at a target. All five arrows are clustered tightly in the upper-left corner, far from the bullseye. A second archer's arrows are scattered all over the target but the average position is near the bullseye. Which archer is more precise and which is more accurate?

options.text: The first archer is both more accurate and more precise
Neither is precise or accurate
The second archer is both more accurate and more precise
The first archer is more precise (tight cluster) but less accurate (far from center); the second has higher accuracy on average but lower precision

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision = consistency (tight cluster = first archer). Accuracy = closeness to the true value / target (average near bullseye = second archer). This classic analogy illustrates that precision and accuracy are independent properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student adds three measurements: 12.1 g + 0.035 g + 1.44 g = 13.575 g. If significant figures rules are applied correctly, what should the reported sum be?

options.text: 13.58 g — round to two decimal places
13.6 g — in addition, round to the fewest decimal places (1 decimal place, from 12.1)
14 g — round to the fewest significant figures
13.575 g — keep all digits

quiz_questions.explanation: In addition, the result is rounded to the fewest decimal places of any measurement. 12.1 has 1 decimal place, 0.035 has 3, and 1.44 has 2. The least is 1; round to 13.6 g.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lab group measures the boiling point of water at their altitude and gets: 99.2, 95.1, 99.3, 99.1, 99.2°C. The accepted value at their altitude is 99.2°C. The 95.1°C reading is dramatically different from the others. What should the group do?

options.text: Identify 95.1°C as an outlier likely caused by an error, investigate its cause, and exclude it if an error is confirmed — the remaining measurements are both accurate and precise
Throw away all data and start over
Report only the 95.1°C value since it is unique
Include all values and report the average including 95.1°C

quiz_questions.explanation: The 95.1°C reading is an outlier — likely caused by an experimental error (e.g., thermometer not fully immersed). After identifying the cause, it can be excluded. The remaining four values (99.1–99.3) are both accurate and precise.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ruler measures to the nearest 0.1 cm. A student writes a measurement as 25 cm instead of 25.0 cm. Why does the missing decimal point matter?

options.text: 25 cm implies 2 significant figures, while 25.0 cm implies 3 — the trailing zero communicates that the measurement is precise to the nearest 0.1 cm
25 cm and 25.0 cm are exactly the same
25 cm means the ruler was used incorrectly
The decimal point makes the number larger

quiz_questions.explanation: Trailing zeros after a decimal point are significant. Writing 25.0 cm (3 sig figs) correctly communicates that the measurement was made to the nearest 0.1 cm. Writing 25 cm (2 sig figs) implies it was only measured to the nearest 1 cm, misrepresenting the instrument's capability.

quiz_questions.question_text: An experiment produces a measured value of gravity g = 9.6 m/s². The accepted value is 9.81 m/s². What is the approximate percent error, and what does it tell us?

options.text: About 0.21% — divide the difference by 100
Percent error cannot be negative, so it equals exactly 2.0%
About 2.1% — calculated as |9.6 − 9.81| / 9.81 × 100% ≈ 2.1%, indicating the measurement is reasonably close to the accepted value
About 21% — the difference is 0.21

quiz_questions.explanation: |9.6 − 9.81| / 9.81 × 100% = 0.21/9.81 × 100% ≈ 2.14%. This relatively small percent error suggests the measurement technique was reasonably good, though some systematic or random error is present.
